the king in yellow is a collection of short stories that connects with each other by the play of The King in Yellow

Chambers borrowed the names Carcosa, Hali and Hastur from Ambrose Bierce: specifically, his short stories "An Inhabitant of Carcosa" and "Haïta the Shepherd". There is no strong indication that Chambers was influenced beyond liking the names. For example, Hastur is a god of shepherds in "Haïta the Shepherd", but is implicitly a location in "The Repairer of Reputations", listed alongside the Hyades and Aldebaran.

Lovecraft read The King in Yellow in early 1927 and included passing references to various things and places from the book—such as the Lake of Hali and the Yellow Sign—in "The Whisperer in Darkness" (1931)
Hastur is only mentioned in “The Whisperer in Darkness” if we only count Lovecraft work
so yeah they just borrow the name Hastur from Bierce and turns him into King in Yellow
Hastur in “Haïta the Shepherd” is a benevolent god but Hastur in Mythos is like the opposite

In Whisperer, Lovecraft also seems to treat Hastur as a place rather than an entity (he never indenifies Hastur as being the same as "He Who Shall Not be Named" either), which isn't inconsistent with Repairer.
Its August Derleth who made Hastur into a Great Old One via Return of Hastur, one of his few Mythos stories that he worked on while Lovecraft was alive and with his direct consultation

well Derleth does address this through his elemental system. Cthulhu and Hastur are rival brothers in his work so plenty of humans are known to call upon about Hastur and his servants for aid against Cthulhu.

This is the same kind of logic as Lovecraft fans use against Derleth's elemental system though. It doesn't make sense to treat him as water elemental when the _ocean_ was Cthulhu's prison. There's no concept of a Elder Sign locked gate in Call of Cthulhu or whatever, Cthulhu is trapped in R'lyah because he's under the ocean
